coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor for visual detection of chlortetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan
Chen, Qianlan
Li, Shuzhen
Cao, Yichuan
Zhang, Bo
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: The development of a highly selective and sensitive method for detecting chlortetracycline (CTC) is crucial for safeguarding public health and food safety. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor called SiC@ZIF-8@MIP was constructed to specifically recognize and sensitively detect CTC. The sensor has the advantages of fast response speed (7 min), wide linear range (0.1–18 μg mL−1), and low limit of detection (4.56 ng mL−1). With the addition of CTC, the fluorescence of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP is quenched at 410 nm due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) and a new fluorescence signal is generated at 515 nm by CTC due to the aggregation induced emission effect (AIE). Additionally, for rapid on-site detection of CTC, a smartphone is applied to digitize fluorescence images of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, helping individuals read and analyze the images. This detection method is a promising strategy for on−site assessments of food safety and public health safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Metal organic framework
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Fluorescence sensing detection
Chlortetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of CTC in actual samples
Mechanism of fluorescence sensing of CTC
Evaluation of material selectivity and stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) ZIF-8, (B) SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and (C) SiC@ZIF-8@NIP. TEM images of (D) ZIF-8, (E) SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and (F) SiC@ZIF-8@NIP. The FTIR spectra of ZIF-8, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP (G). XRD patterns of ZIF-8, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP (H). Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of ZIF-8, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, SiC@ZIF-8@NIP (I).

(A) Fluorescence response of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP at different contact times. (B) Fluorescence response of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP toward different concentrations of CTC. (C) Comparison of the linear calibration plot for SiC@ZIF-8@MIP with that for SiC@ZIF-8@NIP. (D) CIE chromaticity coordinates of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP upon the addition of diverse concentrations of CTC. (Different concentrations of CTC were selected as 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18 μg mL−1).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of SiC QDs, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@MIP with CTC. (B) UV − vis absorption spectrum of CTC and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP. (C) Fluorescence emission decay curves of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP with CTC and (D) SiC@ZIF-8@MIP alone.

(A) Effect of CTC, OTC, DOX, CAP, and SMZ (10 μg mL−1) binding on SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP. (B) The effect of OTC, DOX, CAP and SMZ (50 μg mL−1) on the binding of CTC (10 μg mL−1) to SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP. (C) Influence of coexisting interferents (50 μg mL−1) on the fluorescence intensity ratio of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP in the absence or presence of CTC (10 μg mL−1). (D) The effect of irradiation time at 365 nm on the fluorescence intensity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP. (E) The effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP in the presence of CTC (10 μg mL−1).

Detection of different concentrations of CTC and their linear fitting curves using a smartphone color recognition system (Different concentrations of CTC were selected as 0.05, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15 μg mL−1).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and detection of CTC.
Determination of CTC in real samples using SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor for visual detection of chlortetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Bioengineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Bioengineering
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Bioengineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of a highly selective and sensitive method for detecting chlortetracycline (CTC) is crucial for safeguarding public health and food safety. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor called SiC@ZIF-8@MIP was constructed to specifically recognize and sensitively detect CTC. The sensor has the advantages of fast response speed (7 min), wide linear range (0.1–18 μg mL−1), and low limit of detection (4.56 ng mL−1). With the addition of CTC, the fluorescence of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP is quenched at 410 nm due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) and a new fluorescence signal is generated at 515 nm by CTC due to the aggregation induced emission effect (AIE). Additionally, for rapid on-site detection of CTC, a smartphone is applied to digitize fluorescence images of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, helping individuals read and analyze the images. This detection method is a promising strategy for on−site assessments of food safety and public health safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a popular tetracycline antibiotic that is extensively used to treat bacterial infections in humans and animals and is as a supplement to promote the growth of livestock (Li & Sun, 2022; Yan et al., 2023). However, inadequate rest periods and unreasonable use have resulted in residues of CTC in ecosystems as well as in food of animal origin, causing long-term adverse effects on non − target organisms and the environment. (Meng et al., 2023). The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Union (EU) have set MRLs for CTC in milk at 676 nM and 225 nM, respectively (Yang et al., 2022). The development of a highly sensitive and selective detection method to effectively monitor CTC levels is urgently needed (Li, Li, et al., 2024).

Traditional chromatography provides favorable specificity and sensitivity but tend to require expensive instrumentation, complex and time − consuming sample pretreatment processes, skilled technicians, and so on (Wang, Li, et al., 2024). In contrast to it, fluorescence analysis method shows several advantages, including convenient operation steps, high sensitivity, excellent stability, and ease of visual analysis (Taskiran et al., 2024). Ratiometric fluorescence methods usually have two independent fluorescence signals and utilize the relative ratio of the intensities between the two fluorescence emission peaks to detect analytes. Compared to a single fluorescence signal, background absorption can be effectively avoided, and errors can be reduced (Chi et al., 2023). The brilliant self−calibration feature of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor significantly improves the detection sensitivity. The chromaticity change in the dual fluorescence signal can easily be recognized by the naked eye, which enhances the accuracy of visual detection (Bu et al., 2023; Zhang, Wang, et al., 2024). In recent years, smartphones have attracted growing attention due to their portability, ease of operation, etc. With a smartphone camera and professional color recognition software, the fluorescence color change can be quickly identified to achieve rapid on−site detection of analytes (Li, Liu, et al., 2024).

Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is created by combining functional monomers and crosslinkers with template molecules to generate binding sites that perfectly match the shape, size, and functional groups of template molecules (Gong et al., 2024; Xie et al., 2024). Besides their ability to recognize, MIP has excellent physical and chemical stability, high affinity, ease of preparation and low cost (Hasaneen et al., 2024). In recent years, molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors can be constructed with high sensitivity and high selectivity by combining novel fluorescence materials such as carbon dots (CDs) and quantum dots (QDs), with molecularly imprinted technology (Li et al., 2022). In comparison to CD − based fluorescence sensors, MIP as a specific recognition element improves the ability of sensors to detect pesticide and veterinary drug residues in complex food matrices (Dai et al., 2023).

Inspired by the above, an efficient molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor called SiC@ZIF-8@MIP was constructed in this work (Scheme 1 ). The precursor fluorescence probe SiC@ZIF-8 was created by combining the abundant amino groups on the surface of SiC QDs with Zn2+ in ZIF-8 through ligand interactions (Miao et al., 2022). ZIF-8 exhibited a high specific surface area and favorable stability, and its introduction can significantly enhance the adsorption capacity for the target, maintain the dispersion of SiC QDs, and improve the mass transfer rate of the fluorescence sensor. The ratiometric molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor with specific recognition of CTC was constructed based on the internal filtering effect (IFE) and aggregation−induced emission (AIE), in which the fluorescence emission of SiC QDs was burst at 410 nm and the fluorescence of CTC was enhanced at 515 nm. Moreover, the potential application of the fluorescence sensor was verified by detecting CTC in milk and pig liver. To further extend the application prospects of the constructed sensors, a smartphone−assisted visual detection method was developed, providing an effective strategy to rapid detect hazardous substances in food.

Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 2 − methylimidazole (2 − MI) were purchased from Macklin Biochemicals Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). 3 − aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DOX), sulfamerazine (SMZ), and chloramphenicol (CAP) were purchased from TCI Development Company (Shanghai, China). All experimental procedures involved the utilization of double distilled water (DDW). The quality of all chemical reagents was determined to be analytical.

Fluorescence tests during the experiments were measured by Hitachi F − 7100 (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV − vis) absorption spectra were detected by a UV − 3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The surface functional groups of the materials were determined by a Tensor−37 flourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The morphology was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and S4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). The structure of materials was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, Germany), powder X − ray diffraction (XRD, D8 advance, Buker, Germany), and the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. The stability of materials was analyzed by the thermos gravimetry analysis (TGA, TA, USA) at a rate of 10 °C min−1 to 800 °C.

SiC QDs were prepared by hydrothermal method referring to the technique of Qiao et al. (Qiao et al., 2021). Briefly, 40 mg of citric acid was dispersed into 10 mL of DDW and nitrogen blown for 15 min. Then 2 mL of APTES was injected and stirred thoroughly for 15 min. And then the above solution was reacted in Teflon liner at 200 °C for 2 h. After naturally cooled, the yellowish solution was filtered by using a 0.22 μm filter membrane and subsequently dialyzed for 20 h. Finally, the obtained SiC QDs were preserved at 4 °C for later use.

ZIF-8 was synthesized with some modifications by referring to the method of Liu et al. (X. Liu et al., 2023). Firstly, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (1 mmol) and 2 − MI (4 mmol) were weighed and respectively dissolved into 15 mL of methanol by ultra−sounding for 15 min. The two solutions were immediately mixed and stirred for 1 h, then left to stand for one night. Subsequently, the white precipitate was centrifuged and washed with methanol repeatedly. The final ZIF-8 was obtained by vacuum drying.

The sol−gel method was used into the preparation of the SiC@ZIF-8@MIP. Typically, the pre − synthesized ZIF-8 (30 mg) was homogeneously dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol. SiC QDs (500 μL) were added and mixed well with continuous. After stirred for 30 min, 0.4 mmol of APTES (functional monolith), 0.1 mmol of CTC, 1 mmol of TEOS (cross−linking agent), and 1 mL of acetic acid solution (catalyst, 0.1 mM) were sequentially added and stirred for 15 min respectively. Subsequently, nitrogen gas was injected for 15 min to remove the oxygen from the system, after which the mixed solution was gently stirred for 8 h without light. Subsequently, the precipitate was eluted with DDW and ethanol alternately. Then vacuum drying 2 h. SiC@ZIF-8@NIP was synthesized using the same procedure, but without CTC.

In the fluorescence measurement of fluorescence sensors, 1 mg of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP was firstly dispersed homogeneously in 2 mL of ethanol. Then different concentrations of CTC standard solutions were mixed with the former (v/v,1:1). After being mixed for 20 min away from light, the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm was used to measure the fluorescence emission spectra within the 380–580 nm range. Three parallel sets of data were measured for each experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CTC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk and pig liver samples were used to demonstrate the analytical performance of the fluorescence sensing method in real usage scenarios. Randomly purchasing two kinds of food products (commercial milk and fresh pork liver) in a local supermarket. The processing of the actual samples was carried out with reference to the previously reported method with minor modifications (He et al., 2024; Hu et al., 2023). The 1 g milk and pig liver were respectively spiked with different concentrations of CTC (0.4, 1.2, and 2.0 mg kg−1), and then stood for overnight. Subsequently, the samples were added with 5 mL acetonitrile. After vortexing for 2 min, the samples were centrifugated (9000 rpm, 10 min). The supernatants were dried using nitrogen and then redissolved with 1 mL of ethanol. The resulting samples were analyzed using the proposed fluorescence sensor.

Pre − processing of milk and pig liver samples and experimental conditions by HPLC referred to the method of the National Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB 31658.6–2021), and the HPLC−UV detection method was used (Hu et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2024).

The fluorescence color of the constructed SiC@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescence sensing materials changed from blue to yellow−green with the increase of CTC concentration, which can be captured by the smartphone. Therefore, we developed a visual rapid detection device using a smartphone and an ultraviolet light box equipped with a 365 nm UV light. As a signal reader, the professional application “Color Picker” was used to read the RGB values of the sample (R for red; G for green; B for blue). To eliminate the interference of sunlight, the UV light box was turned off and kept dark. At the same time, the position of the sample and the smartphone was fixed in the UV light box to eliminate the distance effect. All images were taken with iPhone 13 pro.

The morphology features and structures of ZIF-8, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP, and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP were charactered by SEM, TEM, FTIR, XRD, Brunauer Emmett−Teller (BET), and TGA (Fig. 1 ). The pure ZIF-8 in Fig. 1A and D exhibited a regular dodecahedral structure, in agreement with literature reported (Zhang X. et al., 2024). SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP formed a rough polymer layer on the surface of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1B and C). The developed fluorescence sensors were formed with a hollow structure, which was ascribed to the hydrolysis of TEOS releasing protons, and etching the ZIF-8 skeleton. (Fig. 1E and F). Elemental mapping analysis of Fig. S1 verified the presence of C, N, Si, O, Zn in the composite material.

To further investigate the composition of functional groups in the composites, the FTIR spectra of ZIF-8, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP were compared in Fig. 1G. The absorption peaks of ZIF-8 at 1586 cm−1, 1421 cm−1 and 1309 cm−1 originated from the C − N bond, the adsorption peaks at 1180 cm−1 and 1141 cm−1 were attributed to the imidazole ring vibration, the adsorption peak at 994 cm−1 originated from the CC bond, and the absorption peak at 422 cm−1 originated from C − Zn (Chen et al., 2021). The characteristic peaks of ZIF-8 weakened in SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and SiC@ZIF-8@NIP, and stretching vibrations originating from Si − O and Si − O − Si appeared at 1049 cm−1, 787 cm−1, and 452 cm−1, which further proved the successful preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers. The diffraction peaks of pure ZIF-8 demonstrated in Fig. 1H were consistent with the reported literature. SiC@ZIF-8@MIP was an amorphous crystalline material, resulting in a typically amorphous XRD pattern with broad peaks (Liu et al., 2023). ZIF-8 showed a typical type I isotherm with a high specific surface area of 1249.51 m2 g−1 (Fig. 1I). After modification of the molecularly imprinted layer, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP had a significantly decreased specific surface area (244.71 m2 g−1), while SiC@ZIF-8@NIP had an even lower specific surface area due to the lack of imprinted pores (156.17 m2 g−1). As indicated in Table S1, the pore volume and pore size of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP were slightly larger than that of SiC@ZIF-8@NIP. As shown in Fig. S2, the thermal stability of the materials was enhanced compared to ZIF-8 after modifying the imprinted layer.

For dynamic adsorption, the response of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescence sensor to CTC gradually stabilized with the increase of response time. Fig. 2A indicated SiC@ZIF-8@MIP can reach balance after 7 min, which meant the sensor can easily capture CTC with outstanding fluorescence sensing performance.

For stationary adsorption, a concentration dependence of sensor was observed by analyzing the fluorescence intensity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP versus the concentration of CTC (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence quenching degree of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP followed the Stern−Volmer equation:
I 515
/
I 410
= 1 +
K SV
C CTC
where I 515 and I 410 represented the fluorescence intensity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP at 515 nm and 410 nm, respectively. K SV was the Stern−Volmer constant. C CTC referred to the CTC concentration. Imprinting factor (IF, IF = K SV, MIP/K SV, NIP), as a measurement index, was employed to evaluate the specific recognition ability of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP to CTC (Yang et al., 2023).

Fig. 2C exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP followed a desirable linear relationship with CTC concentration. The linear equation I 515/I 410 = 0.0832C CTC + 0.1961 (R 2 = 0.9986) in the linear range of 0.1–18 μg mL−1. Meanwhile, the LOD of the fluorescence sensor was 4.56 ng mL−1, calculated as 3σ/slope. Here, σ was the standard deviation of the blank samples (n = 11) (Hu et al., 2022; Li, Ji, et al., 2023). The sensing performance of MIP to OTC was validated by comparing slopes of the corresponding standard linear equations of MIP (I
515 /I
410 = 0.0832 C
CTC + 0.1961, R
2 = 0.9986) and NIP (I
515 /I
410 = 0.0119 C
CTC + 0.2075, R 2 = 0.9767) (Fig. 2C). This calculation showed favorable results with a high IF calculation of 6.99, which further emphasized the specific recognition ability of CTC by SiC@ZIF-8@MIP. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, the fluorescence color of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP shifted from blue to yellow-green with the increase of CTC concentration, which can be easily recognized by the naked eye, and was consistent with the results of the chromaticity coordinates (CIE) (Fig. 2D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence sensing of CTC
ce:section.ce:para: For further investigation of the fluorescence response mechanism of the constructed fluorescence sensor to CTC, the fluorescence spectra and UV − vis spectra were analyzed and discussed. Fig. 3A indicated that the fluorescence spectrum of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP displayed a blue shift in the fluorescence emission peaks in comparison to SiC QDs. The observed blue−shift seemed to be attributed to the interaction between the amino group on the surface of SiC QDs and the carboxyl group of CTC, resulting in the formation of an amide. The presence of the amide group then influenced the blue fluorescence of SiC QDs (Wang, Li, et al., 2024).

After the addition of CTC, a notable weakening of the fluorescence intensity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP at 410 nm was observed, whereas the fluorescence intensity of CTC in ethanol was enhanced at 515 nm. Additionally, a simultaneous change in color from blue to yellowish−green was observed. The quenching mechanism at 410 nm of CTC on the fluorescence sensors was further explored in Fig. 3B. The UV − vis absorption spectrum of CTC overlapped with most of the excitation spectrum of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP. As revealed in Fig. 3C and D, the fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly after SiC@ZIF-8@MIP combined with CTC. This indicated that the fluorescence quenching was mainly due to IFE (Lin et al., 2023).

On the other hand, a fluorescence enhancement at 515 nm was observed after CTC binding. Fig. S4 recorded the UV − vis absorption spectra of CTC alone, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP alone and SiC@ZIF-8@MIP after combining CTC. Obviously, the UV − vis absorption spectra of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP after combining CTC was a combination of the first two, which indicated that there was no generation of new complexes. The fluorescence enhancement phenomenon at 515 nm after binding CTC was attributed to the aggregated emission of CTC. The structure of CTC possessed a π − conjugated six−membered ring, which induced the aggregation of CTC molecules to generate π − π stacking and fluorescent signals through the ligand interactions between Zn2+ and CTC as well as specific adsorption of CTC by the imprinted layer (Li, Ji, et al., 2023; Mohammed Ameen et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of material selectivity and stability
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, structural (OTC and DOX) and functional (CAP and SMZ) analogs of CTC were selected to analyze the selectivity of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP for CTC. Fig. 4A showed that the response of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP to CTC was notably higher than other competing substances, which was since CTC and the imprinted cavities were structurally complementary. The lack of molecular imprinted sites caused lower response values of SiC@ZIF-8@NIP to CTC. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, the constructed sensing system still showed outstanding responsiveness to CTC under the conditions of coexistence of the above four substances and CTC. This further indicated that SiC@ZIF-8@MIP can exclude the interference of other coexisting substances and specifically recognize CTC.

Subsequently, the immunity of the constructed fluorescence sensors was evaluated to cope with the complex matrices that may exist in real sample detection. Fluorescence response of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP to common metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 −), amino acids (Lysine, Tyrosine, L − Phenylalanine, L − Cystine), and carbohydrates (glucose and sucrose) as interferents were investigated in the presence and absence of CTC was indicated in Fig. 4C. The results demonstrated that the interferents still had no significant effect on the fluorescence sensing of CTC by SiC@ZIF-8@MIP at a 5 times concentration higher than that of CTC. The developed SiC@ZIF-8@MIP exhibited remarkable specificity in recognizing CTC, making it suitable for on−site qualitative identification of CTC.

Simultaneously, the stability of the SiC@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescence sensors was determined under long−time storage and short−time UV irradiation. Fig. 4D indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was nearly unchanged after 60 min of continuous UV irradiation, demonstrating that it had desirable anti-photobleaching properties. As depicted in Fig. 4E, the sensors can maintain a satisfactory response for 60 days, indicating that the sensors had an excellent storage stability.

To assess the accuracy and applicability of our fluorescence method for CTC detection in milk and pig liver, we compared its performance with HPLC−UV. No CTC was detected in actual samples when using SiC@ZIF-8@MIP for fluorescence detection, aligning with HPLC results. Spike recovery tests for CTC in milk and liver achieved recoveries of 88.96–95.20% with RSD of 1.22–3.41%, consist with HPLC results (Table 1 ).

Compared to other fluorescence methods for detecting CTC, SiC@ZIF-8@MIP with high sensitivity and selectivity can be obtained by a simple and low-cost synthesis method. As demonstrated in Table S2, the constructed method showed the advantages of low LOD, short response time and wide linear range, which made it a satisfactory detection method.

Considering that the constructed fluorescence detection method required the expensive fluorescence spectrophotometer, we proposed and fabricated a smartphone−integrated intelligent fluorescence sensing platform, which can realize the sample − to−results conversion, simplify the operation process of CTC on−site detection, and reduce the detection cost. As indicated in Fig. 5 , SiC@ZIF-8@MIP and CTC standard solutions were homogeneously mixed and shaken in a clean centrifuge tube for 10 min. The fluorescence images of the mixture under 365 nm UV irradiation were taken in a UV light box with a smartphone, and the RGB values were read using the Color Picker application. With the increase of CTC concentration, the fluorescence color of the constructed SiC@ZIF-8@MIP fluorescence sensor changed from blue to yellow−green under 365 nm UV lamp, and this result was consistent with the CIE results determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence color R − value varied less, B − value gradually decreased, and G − value gradually increased with increasing CTC concentration. In the range of 0.1–15 μg mL−1, the G/B value showed a favorable linear relationship with CTC concentration, with a linear equation of G/B = 0.1022 C CTC + 0.5473 (R
2 = 0.9823), and the LOD was calculated to be 0.07685 μg mL−1. Consequently, the constructed smartphone-assisted optical sensing platform was expected to be applied to the rapid analysis of CTC for food safety management and food quality requirements.

In summary, the core−shell molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor SiC@ZIF-8@MIP based on the effect of IFE and AIE was designed in this work. The combination of SiC QDs, ZIF-8 and MIP facilitated the specific recognition and anti−interference of SiC@ZIF-8@MIP in detecting trace CTC. The constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrated a wide liner range for CTC from 0.1 to 18 μg mL−1, and achieved a low detection limit of 4.56 ng mL−1. The developed detection method displayed satisfactory recovery (88.96–95.20%) with low RSD (1.22–3.41%, n = 3) in the spiked detection of real samples. More importantly, the constructed molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor showed a fluorescence color shift from blue to yellow−green with increasing CTC concentration, providing a detection basis for visualization. A smartphone was used as a chromaticity analyzer and RGB value reader to develop a visual sample − to−results monitoring platform, which reduced the cost of detection, facilitated the detection process, and provided a new strategy for the rapid detection of food safety and public health safety.

Yan Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Qianlan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Shuzhen Li: Validation, Investigation. Yichuan Cao: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Bo Zhang: Investigation. Guozhen Fang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Shuo Wang: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32372433) and Changsha Natural Science Foundation Project (No. kq2202193).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.140774.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bu et al., 2023

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-activated Ratiometric fluorescent Indicator for real-time and visual detection of plasma membrane homeostasis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bu, Y., Wang, H., Deng, Y., Zhong, F., Yu, Z.-p., Zhu, X., & Zhou, H. (2023). Photo-Activated Ratiometric Fluorescent Indicator for Real-Time and Visual Detection of Plasma Membrane Homeostasis. Analytical Chemistry, 95(3), 1838-1846. doi: 10.1021/acs.analchem.2c02793

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An eco-friendly near infrared fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor based on zeolite imidazolate framework-8 for rapid determination of trace trypsin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, S., Fu, J., Zhou, S., Wu, X., Tang, S., Zhao, P., & Zhang, Z. (2021).
